title: Wire FSRS spaced repetition to distributed concepts for long-term retention
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION: A complete FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) implementation exists at lib/interview/srs.ts with ReviewCard, stability, interval, and 4-level rating (again/hard/good/easy). It is NOT connected to any distributed concept.

Wire it: after a user completes a distributed simulation (runs Raft to leader election, merges CRDTs to convergence, etc.), present a quick review card: 'Can you recall: In Raft, how many nodes must vote for a leader to be elected?' User rates their confidence (again/hard/good/easy). SRS schedules the concept for review in N days.

Create ReviewCards for all 11 distributed concepts. Each card has 2-3 key facts (e.g., 'Gossip converges in O(log N) rounds', 'Consistent Hashing moves only 1/N keys when a node is added').

The SRS review prompt should appear on the dashboard or as a notification when a concept is due for review.
acceptanceCriteria: ReviewCards created for all 11 distributed concepts (2-3 facts each)
After completing a simulation, user gets a review prompt
User rates confidence using FSRS 4-level scale
SRS schedules next review based on rating
Due reviews appear on dashboard or as notification

title: Wire streak system and daily micro-challenges for distributed systems
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION: streak-protector.ts has a full micro-challenge system with 30+ questions and time limits. Current categories: caching, load-balancing, databases, messaging, architecture, security, networking, monitoring, scalability, reliability. NO distributed-specific questions.

Add 10+ micro-challenges for distributed systems:
1. 'In a 5-node Raft cluster, what is the minimum number of votes to elect a leader?' (3)
2. 'What does the P in CAP stand for?' (Partition tolerance)
3. 'True or False: In AP mode, reads during partition always return the latest write.' (False)
4. 'Which CRDT operation resolves conflicts using timestamps?' (LWW-Register)
5. 'How many gossip rounds does it take to reach ~100 nodes?' (~7, O(log N))
6. 'What phase comes after PREPARE in 2PC?' (VOTE/COMMIT/ABORT)
7. 'In consistent hashing, adding a node moves approximately what fraction of keys?' (1/N)
8. 'What does a vector clock detect that a Lamport timestamp cannot?' (Concurrency)
9. 'In the Saga pattern, compensating transactions run in what order?' (Reverse)
10. 'What is the main problem with Two-Phase Commit?' (It is blocking — coordinator failure blocks all)

Add category 'distributed' to MicroChallenge type. Wire to streak counter.
acceptanceCriteria: 10+ distributed micro-challenges added to question bank
'distributed' added as a category
Daily challenge rotation includes distributed questions
Completing distributed daily challenge counts toward streak
Questions match concepts in the distributed module

title: Add 10 distributed-specific achievements to gamification system
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION: achievements.ts has 20+ achievements for system design. ZERO for distributed systems.

Add achievements:
1. 'Consensus Master' (mastery) — Complete all Raft scenarios (election, crash, replication)
2. 'CAP Scholar' (learning) — Try all 3 CAP modes with partition injection
3. 'Gossip Guru' (mastery) — Achieve gossip convergence with a killed node
4. 'CRDT Wizard' (mastery) — Merge all 4 CRDT types and resolve a conflict
5. 'First Election' (learning) — Watch your first Raft leader election
6. 'Partition Survivor' (exploration) — Inject and heal a network partition
7. 'Protocol Polyglot' (exploration) — Visit all 11 distributed simulations
8. 'Speed Convergence' (mastery) — Achieve gossip convergence in under 5 rounds
9. 'Distributed Thinker' (streak) — Complete 7 consecutive daily distributed challenges
10. 'Teaching Machine' (rare) — Correctly predict 10 consecutive simulation outcomes

Each with XP reward, icon, and rarity level.
acceptanceCriteria: 10 distributed achievements added to ACHIEVEMENTS array
Each has id, name, description, icon, category, condition, xpReward, rarity
Achievement triggers fire from distributed module interactions
Achievements appear in user profile

title: Wire ShareDialog + RecordButton to distributed simulations
description:
LAYER 7 COMMUNITY + LAYER 9 DISCOVERY: ShareDialog.tsx and RecordButton.tsx exist as shared components but are NOT connected to the distributed module.

ShareDialog: Add a 'Share' button to the distributed module status bar that opens ShareDialog with a deep-link URL encoding current simulation + state (e.g., ?sim=raft&step=42). Requires DIS-111 (deep-link routing) first.

RecordButton: Add record functionality to capture the canvas animation as a GIF or video. When a user clicks Record, starts capturing. Click again to stop. Export as GIF for sharing on Twitter/social.

These two features together make visualizations SHAREABLE — the key virality mechanism.
acceptanceCriteria: Share button visible in distributed module
Share generates URL with simulation state encoded
Record button captures canvas animation
Export as GIF works for at least Raft and Gossip animations
Social preview (OG image) works for shared URLs

title: Add distributed-specific skill tree nodes to existing skill tree system
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION: skill-tree.ts has a 'distributed-systems' track. Check if nodes match our 11 simulations. If not, add/update nodes:

Distributed Systems track (10 nodes):
1. 'CAP Theorem' (column 0) — prereqs: none
2. 'Lamport Timestamps' (column 0) — prereqs: none
3. 'Vector Clocks' (column 1) — prereqs: lamport-timestamps
4. 'Consistent Hashing' (column 1) — prereqs: cap-theorem
5. 'Gossip Protocol' (column 2) — prereqs: cap-theorem
6. 'CRDTs' (column 2) — prereqs: vector-clocks, gossip
7. 'Two-Phase Commit' (column 2) — prereqs: cap-theorem
8. 'Saga Pattern' (column 3) — prereqs: two-phase-commit
9. 'Raft Consensus' (column 3) — prereqs: cap-theorem, vector-clocks
10. 'Paxos' (column 4) — prereqs: raft

Each node unlockable by exploring the corresponding simulation + answering 1 SRS question correctly.
acceptanceCriteria: Distributed-systems track has nodes for all 11 simulations
Prerequisite DAG matches learning order
Nodes unlock by completing corresponding simulation
Skill tree visual shows distributed track correctly

title: Add distributed Design Battles: head-to-head consensus challenge
description:
LAYER 7 COMMUNITY: design-battles.ts has Elo-based PvP battles for system design. Adapt for distributed: two players race to achieve consensus fastest.

Battle challenge: 'Consensus Race' — Both players get a 5-node cluster with random initial state. Who can get to a committed command first? Player controls: crash nodes, send commands, manage partitions. Timer runs. First to commit wins.

This is novel because distributed systems battles don't exist anywhere — every other platform does coding races, not protocol races.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 2 distributed-specific battle challenges
Each battle uses the Raft or CAP engine
Elo rating updated after each battle
Results appear on leaderboard

title: Add Time Attack mode for distributed: 'Converge in N Rounds'
description:
LAYER 7 COMMUNITY: time-attack.ts has timed challenges for system design canvas. Adapt for distributed: timed challenges with specific goals.

Challenges:
1. 'Fast Election' — Get a Raft leader elected in minimum ticks. Score = fewer ticks = better.
2. 'Gossip Sprint' — Converge 8 nodes with fanout=2 in fewest rounds.
3. 'Partition Healer' — Write data, inject partition, write on both sides, heal, reconcile — fastest time wins.
4. 'CRDT Merge Master' — Create a conflict on all 4 CRDT types and resolve. Fastest wins.

Scored on time + correctness. Results go to leaderboard.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 distributed time-attack challenges
Timer visible during challenge
Score based on time + correctness
Results saved to leaderboard

title: Add sonification: hear the difference between O(log N) and O(N) convergence
description:
LAYER 10 INNOVATION (never-been-done): Use Web Audio API to sonify distributed protocol behavior. Each gossip exchange = a note. Each Raft vote = a chord. Convergence = harmony resolving.

The pitch-based encoding makes O(log N) vs O(N) convergence AUDIBLE: gossip with fanout=3 sounds like a rapid crescendo (exponential), while fanout=1 sounds like slow individual notes (linear).

Raft election: uncertainty phase = dissonant low tones. Votes arriving = ascending notes. Leader elected = major chord resolution. Crash = jarring percussion.

This would be a FIRST IN THE WORLD: no distributed systems tool has ever used audio to teach protocol behavior. It engages an entirely untapped sensory channel and is deeply accessible (blind users can hear the protocol working).
acceptanceCriteria: Web Audio API generates sounds during Raft and Gossip simulations
Sound toggle (on/off) in module controls
Gossip: note per exchange, pitch rises as convergence approaches
Raft: chord on election, percussion on crash
Sound disabled by default (opt-in)
Respects prefers-reduced-motion (no sound in reduced motion mode)

title: Add 'Invent Your Own Protocol' sandbox mode
description:
LAYER 10 INNOVATION (Bloom's Level 6: Create): Let users design their own simple consensus protocol by wiring together primitives: 'on timeout → send vote request', 'on receiving majority → become leader', 'on leader crash → restart election'.

Provide building blocks (visual nodes representing protocol rules) and a test harness that injects failures to see if the user's protocol survives. This is the ultimate learning exercise: if you can DESIGN a protocol that handles failures, you truly understand distributed systems.

MVP: a simplified rule editor with 5-6 primitives (send message, receive message, timeout, check majority, change role). The engine executes the rules on a simulated cluster. User sees if their protocol achieves consensus or deadlocks.
acceptanceCriteria: User can define 5-6 protocol rules from primitives
Engine executes rules on a simulated cluster
Failure injection tests the protocol
Clear feedback: 'Your protocol achieved consensus in 12 ticks' or 'Deadlock detected'
Pre-built 'Raft-like' template as starting point

title: Add 'Failure Injection Race': who can break the system fastest?
description:
LAYER 10 INNOVATION (inversion: failure as the GOAL): Instead of building resilient systems, challenge users to BREAK them. Given a running distributed system, inject the minimum number of failures to cause data loss, split-brain, or deadlock.

The twist: the FEWER failures you inject, the higher your score. This teaches students to think like adversaries — understanding exactly which failure mode breaks each protocol.

Levels:
1. Break a 5-node Raft cluster (crash majority → no leader election possible)
2. Cause data divergence in AP mode (partition → write both sides)
3. Create a deadlock in 2PC (crash coordinator after PREPARE)
4. Prevent gossip convergence (kill nodes strategically)

This gamifies failure analysis — the core skill that separates senior from junior distributed systems engineers.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 4 failure injection challenges
Scoring: fewer injected failures = higher score
Clear success condition per challenge (data loss detected, deadlock detected, etc.)
Leaderboard for 'most efficient breaker'
Explanation after each challenge: 'This is called [failure mode]. Here's how production systems prevent it.'

title: Add 'Distributed Systems Wrapped': annual/weekly learning recap
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION (Spotify Wrapped model): Generate a shareable visual summary of the user's distributed systems learning journey.

Weekly recap: 'This week you explored 4 protocols, crashed 12 nodes, achieved 3 convergences, and correctly predicted 7/10 outcomes. Your best topic: CRDTs. Your weakest: Paxos — try it again!'

Annual recap: 'In 2026 you mastered 8/11 distributed concepts, maintained a 23-day streak, and ranked in the top 15% of distributed systems learners.'

Shareable as an image (OG-card-style) for Twitter/LinkedIn. This is the #1 organic growth mechanic — people SHARE their learning summaries.
acceptanceCriteria: Weekly recap generated from progress data
Shows: protocols explored, nodes crashed, predictions correct, streak
Highlights strongest and weakest topics
Shareable as image (downloadable or social-card)
Appears on dashboard or as weekly notification

title: Add structured data (JSON-LD) to distributed concept pages for SEO
description:
LAYER 9 DISCOVERY: Concept pages at /concepts/[slug] exist for distributed topics but have no structured data. Google uses JSON-LD schema.org markup to display rich results.

Add LearningResource schema to each distributed concept page:
```json
{
  "@type": "LearningResource",
  "name": "Raft Consensus — Interactive Visualization",
  "description": "...",
  "educationalLevel": "advanced",
  "learningResourceType": "interactive simulation",
  "about": { "@type": "Thing", "name": "Raft consensus algorithm" },
  "provider": { "@type": "Organization", "name": "Architex" }
}
```

This helps Google understand the content is educational and display it with rich snippets.
acceptanceCriteria: JSON-LD LearningResource on all distributed concept pages
educationalLevel matches concept difficulty
Google Rich Results Test validates the markup
Sitemap includes all concept pages

title: Create comparison landing pages for high-traffic search terms
description:
LAYER 9 DISCOVERY: High-traffic search terms include 'Raft vs Paxos comparison', 'CAP theorem explained', '2PC vs Saga pattern', 'consistent hashing explained'. Creating dedicated landing pages for these terms with embedded interactive simulations would drive organic traffic.

Create 3 comparison pages:
1. /learn/raft-vs-paxos — side-by-side interactive comparison
2. /learn/2pc-vs-saga — when to use which
3. /learn/cap-theorem-explained — interactive CAP explorer

Each page: SEO-optimized title/description, embedded simulation, rich educational content from concepts-data.ts, interview questions, CTA to full platform.
acceptanceCriteria: 3 comparison landing pages created
Each embeds the relevant interactive simulation
SEO meta tags optimized for target search terms
OG images for social sharing
Internal links to concept pages and full platform

title: Wire Socratic Tutor to distributed simulations for proactive guided learning
description:
LAYER 6 RETENTION: ai/socratic-tutor.ts has conversation flows for system design topics. NOT connected to distributed simulations.

Add proactive Socratic prompts that trigger during simulation use:
- After Raft election: 'You just saw an election. What would happen if TWO nodes timed out simultaneously? Try crashing the leader and a follower at the same time.'
- After CAP partition: 'You're in CP mode. What would AP mode do differently with this same partition? Toggle the mode to find out.'
- After CRDT merge: 'Both replicas had value 3 and 5. Why did the merge result in 5, not 8? (Because G-Counter uses MAX, not SUM.)'

The tutor should ask questions that guide discovery, not give answers. Appears as a subtle sidebar prompt, not a modal.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 1 Socratic prompt per simulation (11 total)
Prompts trigger after specific meaningful moments
Prompts guide to discovery (questions, not answers)
User can dismiss or disable
Prompts use existing Socratic tutor infrastructure

title: Add embeddable iframe widget for distributed simulations (blog/docs embedding)
description:
LAYER 9 DISCOVERY: No embeddable widget exists. Blog authors, professors, and documentation sites should be able to embed a specific simulation.

Create /embed/distributed/[sim-id] route that renders ONLY the canvas + minimal controls (no sidebar, no properties panel). Include an 'Embed' button in ShareDialog that generates:
```html
<iframe src='https://architex.dev/embed/distributed/raft' width='600' height='440'></iframe>
```

This is how tools like Desmos, CodePen, and Observable grow: embedded content drives traffic back to the full platform.
acceptanceCriteria: Embed route renders simulation-only view
Minimal controls (play/pause/reset only)
Architex branding with 'Open in Architex' link
iframe code copyable from ShareDialog
Responsive within iframe dimensions
